column1: #separator:tab
What does XOR represent in binary addition?
column2: The sum of two bits without carry — 1^1=0, 1^0=1, 0^0=0.

column1: How do you compute the carry in bitwise addition?
column2: (a & b) << 1 — AND finds where both bits are set, left shift moves the carry to the correct position.

column1: Why is a loop needed in the bitwise addition approach?
column2: XOR and AND+shift produce two new numbers that still need to be added; repeat until AND (carry) becomes 0.

column1: Time and space complexity of bitwise addition (no + operator)?
column2: O(1) time and space — bounded by fixed 32-bit integer width.

column1: What is the DP recurrence for Counting Bits?
column2: bits[i] = 1 + bits[i - p], where p is the largest power of 2 ≤ i.

column1: What pattern do sections between powers of 2 follow in bit counts?
column2: Each section [2^k, 2^(k+1)-1] mirrors the previous section with every value incremented by 1.

column1: Time and space complexity of the DP approach for Counting Bits?
column2: O(n) time and O(n) space.

column1: When should you look for power-of-2 boundaries instead of constant intervals?
column2: When counting or comparing bit properties — bit patterns reset and mirror at powers of 2, not at constant steps.

column1: How do you extract the least significant bit of a number?
column2: n & 1 — AND with 1 isolates the last bit.

column1: In Reverse Bits, why is revNum left-shifted before OR-ing the current bit?
column2: To make room at the LSB — existing bits shift left, then the new extracted bit is OR-ed into position.

column1: What are the shift directions for input vs output in Reverse Bits?
column2: Input (n) is right-shifted to expose each bit; output (revNum) is left-shifted to build the reversed number.

column1: Time and space complexity of iterative bit reversal?
column2: O(1) — exactly 32 iterations, no extra space.

column1: What are the three cases when inserting an interval into a sorted non-overlapping list?
column2: Entirely before (cur.end < new.start), entirely after (cur.start > new.end), or overlapping — merge by expanding newInterval bounds.

column1: How do you detect that two intervals do NOT overlap?
column2: They don't overlap if cur.end < new.start (current is before) or cur.start > new.end (current is after).

column1: How do you merge an overlapping interval into newInterval?
column2: newInterval.start = min(newInterval.start, cur.start); newInterval.end = max(newInterval.end, cur.end).

column1: Time and space complexity of insert interval?
column2: O(n) time, O(n) space for the output array.

column1: Why must intervals be sorted before merging?
column2: Sorting by start time ensures all potentially overlapping intervals are adjacent, enabling a single linear pass.

column1: What is the overlap condition in Merge Intervals?
column2: cur.start <= prevEnd — current interval starts at or before the last merged interval ends.

column1: How do you extend a merged interval when overlap is found?
column2: ans.back()[1] = max(ans.back()[1], curEnd) — take the further of the two end points.

column1: Time and space complexity of Merge Intervals?
column2: O(n log n) for sorting, O(n) space for output.

column1: In Non-Overlapping Intervals, which interval should you remove when two overlap?
column2: Remove the one with the larger end — keeping the smaller end greedily reduces the chance of future overlaps.

column1: Why don't you need to actually delete intervals from the array?
column2: You only need the removal count; track prevEnd virtually and increment a counter when overlap is detected.

column1: What is the overlap condition in Non-Overlapping Intervals?
column2: cur.start < prevEnd — strict less than, because touching intervals [1,2],[2,3] are not overlapping.

column1: Time and space complexity of Non-Overlapping Intervals?
column2: O(n log n) for sorting, O(1) extra space.

column1: What data structure gives the optimal O(n log n) solution for Meeting Rooms II?
column2: A min-heap of end times — always check if the earliest-ending room is free before allocating a new one.

column1: What is the condition to reuse an existing room in Meeting Rooms II?
column2: cur.start >= heap.top() — the meeting starts at or after the earliest-finishing room is free.

column1: Why does scanning a vector of rooms give O(n²) instead of O(n log n)?
column2: Finding the minimum end time in a vector is O(n) per interval; a min-heap reduces this to O(log n).

column1: Time and space complexity of the optimal Meeting Rooms II solution?
column2: O(n log n) time for sorting and heap operations, O(n) space for the heap.

column1: What is the simplest O(n) approach for Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock?
column2: Track minPrice seen so far; for each price, maxProfit = max(maxProfit, price - minPrice).

column1: When should the buy pointer advance in this problem?
column2: When the current price is lower than the current buy price — reset buy to the current position.

column1: Why initialise maxProfit to 0 and not nums[0]?
column2: You can always choose not to transact, so 0 is the minimum possible profit.

column1: Time and space complexity of Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock?
column2: O(n) time, O(1) space.

column1: What is the core decision in Kadane's algorithm at each element?
column2: Extend the current subarray (curSum + nums[i]) or start fresh (nums[i]) — whichever is larger.

column1: Kadane's recurrence for Maximum Subarray?
column2: curSum = max(curSum + nums[i], nums[i]); maxSum = max(maxSum, curSum).

column1: Why must maxSum be initialised to nums[0] and not 0?
column2: For all-negative arrays, 0 would be wrongly returned; the actual answer is the least-negative element.

column1: Time and space complexity of Kadane's algorithm?
column2: O(n) time, O(1) space.
